one_liner: A 72-year-old woman presents with 3 weeks of new, severe right-sided headache and pain in her jaw while chewing. This morning she experienced a brief episode of vision loss in her right eye.

stage2.hpi_extension: The headache is localized over the right temple and is described as constant and throbbing. She notes pain in her jaw muscles after a few minutes of chewing that forces her to stop eating. Combing her hair is uncomfortable because her scalp is tender. This morning she experienced a transient curtain-like loss of vision in her right eye (amaurosis fugax) lasting about 10 minutes. For the last 2 months she has also had bilateral shoulder and hip girdle morning stiffness, fatigue, low-grade fevers, and a 3-kg weight loss.
stage2.physical_exam: General: elderly woman who looks fatigued. HEENT: right temporal artery is tender, thickened, and pulseless on palpation. Scalp tenderness over the right temporal region. Funduscopic exam of the right eye: mild disc pallor with early edema. Visual acuity: 20/80 OD, 20/25 OS. Shoulders and hips: limited active range of motion due to pain and stiffness without frank weakness. Carotid and peripheral pulses otherwise normal without bruits.

lab_results.result: ESR 112 mm/hr (markedly elevated). CRP 9.8 mg/dL (markedly elevated).
lab_results.clinical_significance: A markedly elevated ESR (often >100 mm/hr) is the hallmark laboratory finding in giant cell arteritis and, in the right clinical context, mandates immediate high-dose corticosteroids to prevent permanent blindness.

lab_results.result: Right temporal artery biopsy (long segment): granulomatous inflammation of the media with multinucleated giant cells, fragmentation of the internal elastic lamina, and intimal thickening. Skip lesions noted.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Temporal artery biopsy remains the gold standard for diagnosis. Because inflammation occurs in skip lesions, a long segment (>1 cm) should be obtained. A negative biopsy does not exclude GCA if the clinical picture is compelling.

lab_results.result: Normocytic normochromic anemia (Hgb 10.8 g/dL). Platelets 480 x 10^9/L (reactive thrombocytosis). WBC normal.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Anemia of chronic inflammation and reactive thrombocytosis are commonly seen in GCA and reflect ongoing systemic inflammation.

stage5.final_clue: She is started on high-dose IV methylprednisolone immediately, before the biopsy is performed. Her headache resolves within 48 hours and her visual symptoms do not recur. Biopsy confirms the diagnosis.

debrief.classic_presentation: Giant cell (temporal) arteritis is a large-vessel vasculitis of adults over 50, strongly favoring women. Classic features include new-onset unilateral temporal headache, jaw claudication (highly specific), scalp tenderness, and visual disturbance ranging from amaurosis fugax to sudden permanent monocular blindness due to anterior ischemic optic neuropathy. Constitutional symptoms (fever, malaise, weight loss) are common, and roughly 50% of patients have coexisting polymyalgia rheumatica.
debrief.pathophysiology: GCA is a granulomatous vasculitis affecting large and medium arteries, most notably branches of the external carotid (including the temporal artery) and the ophthalmic artery. T-cell mediated inflammation with multinucleated giant cells destroys the internal elastic lamina, causing vessel wall thickening, luminal narrowing, and ischemia. Inflammation occurs in a segmental (skip) pattern, which is why long biopsy specimens are required.
debrief.treatment_overview: High-dose corticosteroids (prednisone 40-60 mg daily, or IV methylprednisolone if vision is threatened) must be started immediately without waiting for biopsy results to prevent irreversible blindness. Steroids are slowly tapered over months to a year based on symptoms and ESR/CRP. Tocilizumab (IL-6 inhibitor) is a steroid-sparing option. Low-dose aspirin reduces ischemic complications.
high_yield_pearls: GCA is the most common primary vasculitis in adults and an ophthalmologic emergency due to risk of irreversible blindness.
Start steroids immediately on clinical suspicion; do not wait for the biopsy result.
ESR is typically >50 and often >100 mm/hr.
Jaw claudication is the most specific symptom; temporal artery tenderness is the most specific physical finding.
Roughly half of GCA patients have polymyalgia rheumatica; conversely about 15% of PMR patients develop GCA.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Musculoskeletal, Skin, and Connective Tissue chapter, 'Large-vessel vasculitis — Giant cell (temporal) arteritis' section.
